reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.
reviews.main_review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents HyperDQN as a practical algorithm of Randomized least-square value iteration (RLSVI).  HyperDQN consists of two parametric models: first is a base model that is similar to that of a DQN agent and the second a meta model that parameterizes the last layer of the Q-network as a function of a latent variable. The purpose of this architecture is to generate posterior samples using the diverse Q-value functions represented through this architecture. The paper demonstrates this approach empirically across a variety of Atari games, SuperMarioBros and DeepSea.

Main Review:
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, and also points out reasons why this is non-trivial. Also, the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN are also discussed, which is important because BootDQN is a highly relevant prior work in this domain. Overall the paper is well-written and has strong motivations that are relevant to the RL community at large. 

The following are some of the questions based on my understanding of the paper and would be great if the authors could clarify some of them: 

1. The meta-model in HyperDQN is considered as a linear parameterized model and there is a theoretical justification for this. What would happen to the empirical/theoretical results if the meta-model is a non-linear function of the latent variable z? I am inclined to think that the representation power for the posterior distribution would be better and thus should produce improvements overall.

2. How are the sigma_w and sigma_p parameters chosen in the objective function? Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the Q_{target} (i.e., sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i ) to the one used in HyperDQN? 

3. The Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 is not sufficient to demonstrate the utility of the presented approach. This curve is computed over all the Atari games (some of them require no exploration and some require a complex strategy for exploration). It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on “hard-exploration” and “easy-exploration” games (this classification is presented in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. 

Also, why are the agents only trained for 20M frames? It is conventional in Atari to run the agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018). This makes it easier to compare the performance of HyperDQN wrto the prior methods in Atari. 

A minor comment: why do some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and another starts at a value lower than 0? 

Bellemare, M., Srinivasan, S., Ostrovski, G., Schaul, T., Saxton, D., and Munos, R. Unifying count-based exploration and intrinsic motivation. In NeurIPS, 2016.

Machado, M. C., Bellemare, M. G., Talvitie, E., Veness, J., Hausknecht, M., & Bowling, M. (2018). Revisiting the arcade learning environment: Evaluation protocols and open problems for general agents. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research, 61, 523-562.

4. How many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all the experiments (Atari, Mario and DeepSea)? It would be useful to add this information in the main text. Also, are the error bars reported in each of the presented learning curves?

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I think the contribution would be important to the RL community. My main concerns with the paper is that the presented approach claims to be helpful for exploration but the empirical results are not demonstrating this: One specific way to demonstrate this would be to show and emphasize that HyperDQN is able to achieve better returns on Atari games that are classified to be hard-exploration games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
The paper introduces HyperDQN, a novel deep reinforcement learning algorithm that extends Randomized Least-Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) to deep RL settings. It specifically addresses two key limitations of RLSVI: the reliance on fixed linear features and the computational burden of updating posterior samples with changing features. HyperDQN achieves this by employing a probabilistic hypermodel that generates parameters for the last layer of a Q-value predicting base model. This architecture is designed to generate diverse Q-value function posterior samples efficiently while learning task-relevant features end-to-end. The authors provide a theoretical guarantee for the hypermodel's ability to approximate posterior distributions in the linear case. Empirically, HyperDQN demonstrates superior sample efficiency, outperforming DQN with 10x fewer frames on Atari, and showing strong results on SuperMarioBros and Deep Sea environments against several state-of-the-art baselines. The paper also highlights the importance of "commitment" in exploration, demonstrating that combining HyperDQN with epsilon-greedy policies can degrade performance.

**Main Review:**
The paper begins with a strong motivation towards computationally implementing RLSVI in Deep RL using a base and meta models, clearly articulating the challenges of feature learning and computational efficiency that previously limited RLSVI. This represents a significant contribution to bridging the gap between theoretically grounded exploration and practical deep RL. The proposed architecture, where a hypermodel generates parameters for the Q-network's final layer, is innovative and well-justified by the detailed explanation of trainability issues, such as gradient explosion, encountered with direct extensions of prior hypermodel work to all layers of deep networks. The discussion of the main differences between BootDQN and HyperDQN is also important, given BootDQN's relevance as prior work. Overall, the paper is well-written, clear, and easy to follow, with strong motivations relevant to the RL community at large. The theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) affirming the hypermodel's ability to approximate posterior distributions in the linear case is a notable strength, providing crucial insight into its mechanism, particularly the justification for the `z⊤ξ` noise term. Furthermore, the paper effectively maps HyperDQN's design to key desiderata for efficient exploration, providing insightful discussions, including the detrimental effects of `ϵ`-greedy on HyperDQN's performance, which supports the commitment principle.

While the paper presents a compelling approach with strong empirical results, several critical and minor issues need to be addressed to enhance its rigor and clarity.

**Theoretical Foundation and Rigor:**
A primary concern lies in the methodological rigor for the *deep RL* context. While the theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) is a strong contribution, it applies strictly to the *linear* Bayesian regression case. Its extrapolation to non-linear deep neural networks and temporal difference learning lacks rigorous justification. More importantly, there are profound inconsistencies in the mathematical notation and dimensional analysis within the theoretical sections (Section 4.1, 4.4, Appendix B.3). Specifically, the definition of the hypermodel $f_{\nu}(z) = \nu_w^T z + \nu_b$ and its associated covariance $N(\nu_b, \nu_w^T \nu_w)$ is dimensionally ambiguous and appears incorrect given the stated dimensions of $\nu_w$ and $z$. This fundamental error propagates through the proof of Theorem 1, rendering the theoretical guarantee unreliable. Furthermore, the application of Lemma 3 in the proof of Theorem 1 also appears mathematically unsound. It would be beneficial for the authors to provide a more rigorous theoretical bridge between Theorem 1 and HyperDQN's deep RL application, or to explicitly discuss these limitations and acknowledge why the linear theory is still considered relevant or indicative of behavior in the non-linear setting. Regarding the meta-model, while the paper states a linear hypermodel has "sufficient representation power," the authors should clarify if this is due to inherent sufficiency or if training difficulties of non-linear MLPs (as discussed in Section 4.2) are the primary bottleneck, especially since Appendix C.4 suggests an MLP hypermodel doesn't yield significant gains.

**Parameter Choices and Empirical Contradictions:**
Regarding the choice of parameters, it would be helpful if the authors could clarify how the $\sigma_w$ and $\sigma_p$ parameters are chosen in the objective function. Have the authors considered a baseline where an agent uses an identical artificial noise term for modifying the $Q_{target}$ (i.e., $\sigma_w z^{\top} \epsilon_i$) to the one used in HyperDQN? More critically, the Deep Sea experiments (Table 4) set the noise scales $\sigma_{\omega}$ and $\sigma_p$ to 0.0, despite the theory (Equation 4.1, Assumption 1) explicitly requiring them to be positive. This directly contradicts the theoretical claims for a significant portion of the experimental validation, which is a major concern. Further ablation studies on these noise and prior scales could provide greater insight into their impact.

**Empirical Demonstrations and Protocols:**
While HyperDQN demonstrates impressive sample efficiency, achieving higher human-normalized scores on Atari with 10x fewer frames than vanilla DQN, the Atari performance curves summarized in Fig 3 are not sufficient to fully demonstrate the utility of the presented approach for exploration. The curve is computed over all Atari games, some requiring no exploration and others a complex strategy. It would be more appropriate to show how the agent performs on "hard-exploration" and "easy-exploration" games (as classified in Bellemare et al. 2016) rather than present a summary across all games. This would be a more powerful argument for HyperDQN's exploration capabilities.

Additionally, the agents are trained for 20M frames, whereas it is conventional in Atari to run agents for 200M frames (Machado et al. 2018) for easier comparison with prior methods. For clarity, providing a direct comparison of HyperDQN (20M frames) against DQN (20M frames) in Table 2 (or a similar aggregate table) would be useful, as DQN's 20M performance is currently only implicitly shown in Figure 3.

The paper notes HyperDQN's struggle with extremely sparse reward tasks like Montezuma's Revenge in the appendix. Given its status as a canonical hard exploration problem, this warrants a more prominent discussion in the main body, elaborating on why HyperDQN struggles (e.g., limited feedback for feature learning) and discussing potential synergies with other intrinsic curiosity methods.

**Clarity, Presentation, and Reproducibility:**
*   It is crucial to state how many random seeds were used to produce the learning curves in all experiments (Atari, Mario, DeepSea) in the main text. The use of a low number of random seeds (e.g., 3) for confidence intervals in Atari experiments raises concerns about statistical robustness, and error bars should be reported in each of the presented learning curves.
*   The definition of $Q_{target}(s', z)$ in (4.2) and (A.6) implies a $z$-dependency for the target Q-value. It would be clearer to explicitly state how $z$ is handled for the target network: is the $z$ sampled for the current policy also used for its corresponding target, or is a new $z$ sampled for the target? Clarifying this detail would aid reproducibility and understanding.
*   Minor inconsistencies in presentation include: why some curves in Fig 3 start at 0 and others at a value lower than 0; Figure 1 contains extraneous "✓" marks; the "computation complexity" definition in Figure 6's caption appears to be copy-pasted from a different publication (Osband et al., 2020); inconsistencies in figure labeling (e.g., Figure 3's "Median human-normalized score" versus Table 2's "maximum human-normalized scores"); misstatements of network architecture details (e.g., convolution filter counts in Appendix C.1); and unclear environment preprocessing descriptions. Improving the font size or simplifying Figure 1 would also enhance its readability.
*   Clarifying the fixed nature of the prior hypermodel's feature extractor (as mentioned in Remark 3) earlier in the methodology would improve clarity.
*   Further ablation studies, such as comparing HyperDQN against BootDQN *without* epsilon-greedy on Atari, would provide a cleaner isolation of the commitment benefit.

**Summary Of The Review:**
Overall, the contribution of HyperDQN would be important to the RL community, offering a novel and practical approach to deep RL exploration that effectively addresses key RLSVI limitations. The architecture is innovative, and the empirical results demonstrate strong sample efficiency. However, the paper suffers from significant theoretical inconsistencies and a lack of rigor in bridging its linear theory to deep non-linear applications. My main concerns are that the presented approach, while promising for exploration, does not sufficiently demonstrate its utility on hard-exploration games, and the empirical results are not always presented in a way that facilitates easy comparison with conventional benchmarks. The critical contradiction between theoretical requirements and experimental parameter settings, alongside numerous clarity and presentation issues, diminishes the paper's overall rigor. Addressing these substantial issues would significantly strengthen the manuscript.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes a new randomized exploration method termed HyperDQN. 

It aims to improve the popular Randomized Least Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) method by addressing RLSVI's major limitations of computational burden and its requirement of known good features in advance to adopt the linear setting. 

The principal of HyperDQN is to combine DQN with a probabilistic hypermodel which could generate posterior samples for the parameter values of the weights at the last linear layer of the Q-function. Both the hypermodel and the Q-function could be jointly optimized during training. By sampling the Q-function parameters, it helps the model to select exploratory action sequences.

 Empirical evaluation results are provided in Atari 2600 domain, SuperMarioBros and deep sea. Overall, the proposed method could outperform Bootstrapped DQN, Double DQN and another recent bootstrapping baseline termed OB2I in majority of the testified domains.
reviews.main_review:
$\textbf{Strength}$: 
- This work tackles an important problem of exploiting randomized exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning problems.
- This paper is very well written and easy to follow.
- Overall the method of combining hypermodel with Q-network is quite sound and very general to be applied on deep RL problems.
- The empirical evaluation results are very extensive where relevant baselines are considered for comparison.

$\textbf{Weakness}$:
- Very limited novelty where the idea is from the work [1]. 
- During comparison, only results at intermediate training stage  (i.e., 20M frames)  is presented while the detailed scores for the full training is missing. So it is unclear if the method could really be the next state-of-the-art method for Atari 2600 test suite.
-Only limited number of baselines are considered. There is not decent exploration methods, such as UCB-type exploration or ramdomized exploration with noisy nets, being considered for comparison.  


$\textbf{Detailed comments}$:

1. My primary concern is about the novelty of this paper. I found that both the formulation for hypermodel and the loss function for optimizing the hypermodel is inherited from the existing work [1]. In [1], a more general formulation for the hypermodel has been discussed which includes linear models, neural models and additive prior models. In HyperDQN, a linear hypermodel is adopted where there is nothing new for the method. Thus the contribution of this paper is not in terms of introducing a new method but is to show additional experimental results for an existing method on several deep RL domains. I feel such contribution might not be significant enough to be published as a full paper in ICLR.

2. This paper tackles the policy learning problem from a direction of considering randomized exploration method. It claims the randomized exploration method O2BI as a SOTA exploration method but I do not think so. Generally, the O2BI as well as HyperDQN is only compared with limited baselines when the training is incomplete. I feel that many other variants of UCB-type algorithms, such as noisy net [2] might be able to outperform O2BI or HyperDQN greatly with 200M training budget. For instance, prediction-error based exploration methods could effectively progress on the extremely hard exploration task Montezuma's Revenge while O2BI and HyperDQN make no progress on it. Therefore, a more inclusive exploration methods for discussion/comparison is desired. 

3. The training curves presented in Fig 12 come with no error bars and/or O2BI is missing from the curves.

4. I feel noisy net is also a related randomized exploration model which should be considered for discussion and/or experimental comparison.

[1] HYPERMODELS FOR EXPLORATION
[2] NOISY NETWORKS FOR EXPLORATION

This paper is a well written one with extensive experimental results. 

The novelty of this method is rather limited as the method is exactly the same as the one proposed in a prior work. 

The empirical evaluation results appear to be less convincing to me because the results on Atari 2600 are only shown for intermediate model trained after 20M frames and the full training scores (inferred from the learning curves in Fig 12) appear to be much inferior than another variant of randomized exploration model which is the noisy net.

reviews.text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper proposes a new randomized exploration method termed HyperDQN.

It aims to improve the popular Randomized Least Square Value Iteration (RLSVI) method by addressing RLSVI's major limitations of computational burden and its requirement of known good features in advance to adopt the linear setting.

The principal of HyperDQN is to combine DQN with a probabilistic hypermodel which could generate posterior samples for the parameter values of the weights at the last linear layer of the Q-function. Both the hypermodel and the Q-function could be jointly optimized during training. By sampling the Q-function parameters, it helps the model to select exploratory action sequences.

Empirical evaluation results are provided in Atari 2600 domain, SuperMarioBros and deep sea. Overall, the proposed method could outperform Bootstrapped DQN, Double DQN and another recent bootstrapping baseline termed OB2I in majority of the testified domains.

Main Review:
This paper tackles an important problem of exploiting randomized exploration methods in deep reinforcement learning problems. The proposed method of combining a hypermodel with a Q-network for randomized exploration is an interesting idea, and the architectural choice to apply the hypermodel only to the last layer of the Q-network is a practical solution to training instability issues often encountered with direct extensions of hypermodels to deep networks. The paper also presents empirical results that, at first glance, appear extensive and promising on some benchmarks, particularly in terms of sample efficiency on Atari.

However, despite these initial strengths and the paper's generally clear writing style, the manuscript suffers from a substantial number of critical and minor flaws that collectively undermine its rigor, clarity, and overall contribution.

$\textbf{Weakness}$:
My primary concerns revolve around profound theoretical inconsistencies, limited novelty, insufficient empirical evaluation, and significant presentation and reproducibility issues.

$\textbf{Detailed comments}$:

1.  **Profound Theoretical Flaws and Contradictions**:
    My most significant concern is about the methodological rigor, particularly within the theoretical sections. There are profound inconsistencies in the mathematical notation and dimensional analysis within Sections 4.1, 4.4, and Appendix B.3. Specifically, the definition of the hypermodel $f_{\nu}(z)$ and its associated covariance appears dimensionally ambiguous and incorrect given the stated dimensions of $\nu_w$ and $z$. This fundamental error propagates through the proof of Theorem 1, rendering the theoretical guarantee unreliable, and the application of Lemma 3 in the proof of Theorem 1 also appears mathematically unsound.

    Furthermore, a major contradiction exists between the proposed theory and the experimental setup. For instance, the Deep Sea experiments (Table 4) set the critical noise scales $\sigma_{\omega}$ and $\sigma_p$ to 0.0, despite the theory (Equation 4.1, Assumption 1) explicitly requiring them to be positive. This directly invalidates the theoretical claims for a significant portion of the experimental validation.

    Beyond these specific errors, the theoretical guarantee (Theorem 1) is strictly for Bayesian linear regression. Its extrapolation to non-linear deep neural networks and temporal difference learning lacks rigorous justification, creating a significant theoretical-empirical gap that is not adequately bridged in the main text. While applying the hypermodel to only the last layer is an empirically sound solution to gradient explosion, a deeper theoretical analysis of how this architecture preserves desired posterior approximation properties in deep networks is needed.

2.  **Limited Novelty**:
    Beyond the theoretical issues, the novelty of this paper is quite limited. Both the formulation for the hypermodel and the loss function for optimizing it are inherited from the existing work [1]. In HyperDQN, a linear hypermodel is adopted, and there is nothing conceptually new for the method itself. Thus, the contribution of this paper is not in terms of introducing a novel method, but rather in showing additional experimental results for an existing method on several deep RL domains. I feel such a contribution, especially when coupled with the theoretical flaws, might not be significant enough to be published as a full paper.

3.  **Incomplete and Insufficient Empirical Evaluation**:
    *   **Partial Training Results**: During comparison, only results at an intermediate training stage (i.e., 20M frames) are presented, while detailed scores for the full training (e.g., 200M frames for Atari) are missing. So it is unclear if the method could really be the next state-of-the-art method for the Atari 2600 test suite when compared to methods with larger training budgets.
    *   **Limited Baselines**: Only a limited number of baselines are considered. There is a lack of comparison against other prominent exploration methods, such as UCB-type algorithms, randomized exploration with noisy nets [2], or more recent, high-performing exploration methods like RND, NGU, or Agent57. The claim that the randomized exploration method O2BI is a SOTA exploration method is questionable without a broader comparison. For instance, prediction-error based exploration methods could effectively progress on the extremely hard exploration task Montezuma's Revenge, while HyperDQN, as noted in the appendix, struggles significantly, making no progress on it. This limitation on extremely sparse reward tasks warrants a more prominent discussion in the main text.
    *   **Statistical Robustness**: The use of a low number of random seeds (3) for confidence intervals in Atari experiments raises concerns about the statistical robustness of the reported results.
    *   **Missing Error Bars/Baselines in Curves**: The training curves presented in Fig 12 come with no error bars, and O2BI is missing from some of these curves, further hindering comprehensive comparison.

4.  **Presentation and Reproducibility Issues**:
    Numerous presentation and formatting inconsistencies further detract from the paper's quality and clarity. Figure 1 contains extraneous "✓" marks, and its text labels are quite small, impacting readability. The "computation complexity" definition in Figure 6's caption is evidently copy-pasted from a different publication (Osband et al., 2020), which is a significant oversight. There are inconsistencies in figure labeling (e.g., Figure 3's "Median human-normalized score" versus Table 2's "maximum human-normalized scores"), misstatements of network architecture details (e.g., convolution filter counts in Appendix C.1), and unclear environment preprocessing descriptions. These issues collectively impact the paper's professionalism and reproducibility.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper, while addressing an important problem in deep RL exploration and presenting a seemingly well-written account with promising initial empirical results, suffers from critical fundamental flaws. The theoretical foundation is severely compromised by profound mathematical inconsistencies and direct contradictions between the theory and the experimental setup. This undermines the core claims of the method. Furthermore, the novelty is limited, and the empirical evaluation is incomplete, lacking crucial comparisons and full training data, and is further weakened by issues in statistical robustness and presentation. Given the severity of the theoretical inconsistencies and the numerous presentation flaws, the paper does not meet the standards for publication.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

[2] NOISY NETWORKS FOR EXPLORATION

Summary Of The Review:
This paper is a well written one with extensive experimental results. 

The novelty of this method is rather limited as the method is exactly the same as the one proposed in a prior work. 

The empirical evaluation results appear to be less convincing to me because the results on Atari 2600 are only shown for intermediate model trained after 20M frames and the full training scores (inferred from the learning curves in Fig 12) appear to be much inferior than another variant of randomized exploration model which is the noisy net.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces Synthetic Environments and Synthetic Rewards as a bi-level optimization problem. The main idea is to find SEs and REs such that an RL agent trained under them will do well in a given, known target environment, without further training.
reviews.main_review:
Results:
- In Figure 2, the performance seems very unstable: In particular, the reward for individual agents seems to fluctuate widely and even the average starts drooping after the 50th iteration in the arcobot task. It would be great to know what the source of this instability is. 

Conceptual:
- There are repeated claims in the paper that the synthetic environment "focuses the learning" on the relevant states or learns "representations" of the target environment ("an informed representation of the target environment by
modifying the state distributions to bias agents towards relevant states"). This claim is entirely unclear to me at the moment. In particular, there simply doesn't seem to be any reason that the SE should have much in common with the ground truth environment. The only condition is that _optimal policies_ for the two environments match. In particular, there will be many environments in which the optimal policy is likely much easier to learn than in the original Env. 
For example, you can imagine a state transition dynamic that is entirely random but provides a large reward for the optimal action in each of the given states. Another option is to learn environment dynamics where the optimal action in each state deterministically transitions to an arbitrary next state, such that the agent will see all required states during training. 
It would be great to provide any type of evidence that there is actually semantic meaning in the SE that matches the underlying ground truth environment.  

Other comments:
- In Figure 2, it seems like the legend is switched? I.e. dashed line should be the solved threshold?

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary Of The Paper:**
This paper introduces Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Synthetic Rewards (SRs), also referred to as Reward Networks (RNs), as a novel approach to training Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents. The core idea is to frame this as a bi-level optimization problem, where an inner loop trains an RL agent on a proxy environment or reward function, and an outer loop, utilizing an evolution strategy, optimizes the SE or RN parameters based on the agent's performance in a given, known target environment. The ultimate goal is to enable an RL agent trained under these learned proxies to perform well in the target environment without further training, often with greater efficiency (fewer interactions) than direct training. The authors demonstrate empirical evidence of efficiency gains, robustness to hyperparameter variations, and transferability to unseen RL agents across a range of classic control tasks.

**Main Review:**

This paper presents a very interesting and novel concept for meta-learning proxy environments and reward functions to accelerate RL agent training. The bi-level optimization scheme, employing Evolution Strategies to meta-learn these proxies, is well-articulated, innovative, and offers an agent-agnostic approach. The work provides extensive empirical evidence across various control tasks and RL algorithms, consistently demonstrating significant efficiency gains for agent training, robustness to hyperparameter variations for known agent types, and some degree of transferability to unseen agents. The provision of code and models is commendable for reproducibility, although a critical error in the provided GitHub URL (containing a space) needs immediate correction to ensure this.

**Strengths and Contributions:**

The introduction of Synthetic Environments (SEs) as full proxy models learned through agent performance is a novel contribution that extends beyond traditional model-based RL or reward shaping. The empirical results demonstrate significant reductions in training steps for agents trained on learned SEs and RNs across several classic control tasks (CartPole, Acrobot, Cliff Walking, MountainCarContinuous). The robustness to agent hyperparameter variation is a strong practical advantage, as shown in the paper's figures. Furthermore, the demonstration that SEs and RNs can transfer to unseen agent architectures (e.g., DDQN-trained SEs training Dueling DDQN agents) highlights a key strength, enhancing the potential practical utility of these learned proxies. The qualitative analysis of SE and RN behavior, suggesting that proxies learn 'informed representations' and 'guiding systems' by modifying state and reward distributions, provides valuable insight into *why* these methods might work.

**Areas for Improvement and Clarification:**

While the concept is highly promising, several aspects require further clarification, deeper analysis, or methodological refinement to solidify the paper's claims and practical utility:

**Conceptual Clarity and Evidence:**
The paper repeatedly claims that the synthetic environment "focuses the learning" on relevant states or learns "informed representations" of the target environment. This claim is currently unclear. While the alternative reviews acknowledge the qualitative analysis offering insights into "informed representations" and "guiding systems," the main concern remains: there doesn't seem to be any inherent reason that the SE should have semantic meaning or structural commonality with the ground truth environment. The only strict condition is that *optimal policies* for the two environments match. It's plausible that an SE could achieve this via dynamics much simpler than the original, potentially leading to agents exploiting artifacts rather than learning generalizable policies. For instance, an SE could provide a large reward for optimal actions in specific states, or deterministically transition to required states, without truly modeling the environment's underlying physics. Providing concrete evidence that there is actual semantic meaning in the SE that matches the underlying ground truth environment would significantly strengthen this conceptual claim.

**Scalability and Computational Cost:**
A major limitation acknowledged by the authors is the significant computational cost of learning full SEs. The paper explicitly states that SEs struggled with more complex environments like HalfCheetah and Humanoid due to resource limitations. This significantly curtails the practical utility of SEs for challenging, high-dimensional problems where such proxy benefits would be most impactful. While RNs scale better, the full SE concept's scalability remains a bottleneck. Furthermore, the paper notes that "learning an SE proxy requires more interactions with the real environment than training agents only on the real environment," indicating a high initial cost for the proxy itself. This initial investment must be rigorously justified against the purported long-term benefits for *multiple* subsequent agent trainings. Future work should explicitly address methods to reduce this computational burden or explore more gradient-efficient meta-optimization techniques.

**Robustness and Transferability Nuances:**
While the method is described as agent-agnostic, the empirical results for transferring SEs to TD3 agents show 'limited transferability' and a high failure rate. The paper's admission of "limited transferability" for TD3, followed by selecting "five best SE models" to demonstrate improved performance, can be perceived as a form of cherry-picking that might misrepresent the overall robustness and generalizability of the learned SEs. If only a small subset of models works, the general claim of transferability is weakened. This suggests that while the meta-learning objective is agent-agnostic, the *learned proxy* might still be somewhat biased towards the agent family used during its training. This nuance should be highlighted more prominently in the main text's discussion of contributions.

**Methodological Details and Baselines:**
*   **Figure 2 Instability:** In Figure 2, the performance seems very unstable. The reward for individual agents fluctuates widely, and even the average starts drooping after the 50th iteration in the Acrobot task. The source of this instability should be investigated and clarified.
*   **Figure 2 Legend:** It appears the legend in Figure 2 is switched, with the dashed line likely representing the solved threshold.
*   **Reward Network Objective:** The RN optimization objective `ntr + wsol * max(0, Csol - Cfin)` is introduced without extensive justification for its specific form and fixed weighting. While an appendix studies alternatives, a clearer initial motivation for this composite objective would be beneficial.
*   **Algorithm 1 (NES Update):** The description of the NES update rule in Algorithm 1, specifically Line 8, appears to be a simplified gradient estimate rather than a full Natural Evolution Strategy update, which typically adapts the covariance matrix. This needs clarification and justification, especially given that the noise standard deviation (σ) is listed as an optimized hyperparameter.
*   **Early Stopping Heuristic (Appendix A.1):** The heuristic `| ¯Cd− ¯C2d| / | ¯C2d| ≤ Cdif f` for early stopping agent training is problematic. If `| ¯C2d|` is zero or very close to zero (e.g., in sparse reward environments before any reward is collected), this division is undefined or highly unstable. A more robust formulation is essential.
*   **Comparison to Baselines:** While the paper compares SE-trained agents to agents trained on the real environment, a more comprehensive comparison against state-of-the-art model-based RL methods or other meta-learning approaches for environment generation is largely absent for SEs. The discussion in Appendix D comparing with supervised model learning is limited and inconclusive regarding the underlying reasons for observed differences.

**Technical and Formatting Issues:**
*   **Reproducibility URL:** The GitHub URL provided (`https://github.com/automl/learning environments`) contains a space, rendering it invalid. This is a critical error for reproducibility.
*   **Internal Cross-References:** The paper repeatedly refers to "Section 9" (e.g., in Section 5 and Appendix A.4) which does not exist in the manuscript. This likely refers to Section 5, indicating a significant oversight.
*   **Equation (1) Notation:** The `arg max` operator is missing the variable over which the maximization is performed (e.g., `arg max_θ`).
*   **Figure 1 Clarity:** The data flow in Figure 1 for SEs, particularly the 'freezepolicy' and 'trainagent' interaction, could be clearer to explicitly denote inner vs. outer loop interactions and avoid confusion with real environment interaction claims.
*   **Minor Formatting:** Inconsistent citation style, unrendered accent marks in author names, and confusing table entries (e.g., Table 15/19 'Optimized value' as a dash) should be addressed.

**Summary Of The Review:**
This is a very interesting paper with early results in a potentially impactful direction. The novel concept of learning synthetic environments and reward networks via bi-level optimization offers significant promise for improving RL training efficiency. While the empirical evidence of efficiency gains and robustness on tested environments is noteworthy, the practical utility of the method, particularly for complex environments due to scalability and computational costs, remains somewhat unclear. Furthermore, the claims of general transferability require more nuanced discussion, and several methodological and technical details need refinement for full rigor and clarity. Despite these limitations, the paper presents an intriguing piece of work that opens exciting new research avenues.

**Update:**
Based on the discussion phase and the rebuttal, I believe this paper has enough merit to be published at ICLR, if there is space. It is an intriguing piece of work, even though the practical utility is very unclear at this point. Addressing the aforementioned points, particularly the critical reproducibility error and clarifying the conceptual claims, would significantly strengthen the manuscript.

**Rating:**
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.
reviews.main_review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.
reviews.correctness: 4: All of the claims and statements are well-supported and correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 4: The contributions are significant, and do not exist in prior works.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.

Main Review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

Summary Of The Review:
This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize proxy models—referred to as Synthetic Environments (SEs) and Reward Networks (RNs)—for components of an RL loop that are typically not learned, such as transition dynamics and reward functions defined in the interacting environment. SEs learn both state dynamics and rewards, acting as full environment proxies, while RNs learn to augment or replace rewards, serving as partial proxies. They learn the parameters of these models by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent using a bi-level optimization scheme. An inner loop trains an RL agent on the proxy, and an outer loop, employing an Evolution Strategy (ES), updates the SE or RN parameters based on the agent's performance in the real environment. The research demonstrates that not only can this setup lead to successfully learned proxy parameters, but agents trained exclusively on these learned SEs can solve corresponding real environments, and both SEs and RNs enable more efficient agent training (fewer interactions) compared to training directly on the real environment. The paper also shows that these learned proxies exhibit robustness to hyperparameter variations and can transfer to unseen RL agents, with empirical results suggesting that SEs achieve efficiency by learning informed representations that bias agents towards relevant states.

Main Review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript. This paper introduces a novel and promising approach to meta-learning proxy environments and reward functions for RL, and it presents a strong, well-executed contribution opening exciting new research directions.

1.  **Clarity, Detail, and Reproducibility:** The writing is extremely clear and detailed, making the paper a pleasure to read. I found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations. Furthermore, the authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was set up in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. The paper includes comprehensive reproducibility details (code, HPs, seeds) and a thoughtful ethics statement, reflecting good academic practice. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. This is truly commendable and a great example of what good ML research looks like. I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop.

2.  **Novelty and Empirical Strength:** The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective. It honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning synthetic environment models. The introduction of Synthetic Environments (SEs) as full proxy models learned through agent performance is a novel contribution that goes beyond traditional model-based RL or reward shaping. The bi-level optimization framework with ES for meta-learning the proxies is a sound and flexible approach, allowing for agent-agnostic optimization. The work provides extensive empirical evidence across various control tasks and RL algorithms (CartPole, Acrobot, Cliff Walking, MountainCarContinuous, HalfCheetah), consistently demonstrating significant efficiency gains for agent training, robustness to hyperparameter variations, and transferability to unseen agents. The qualitative analysis offering insights into how SEs learn informed state/reward representations is a valuable contribution, providing insight into *why* these methods work. Additionally, the experiment contrasting SEs with purely supervised dynamics models in Appendix D strengthens the argument for the task-aware meta-learning objective, showing the benefits of their approach over a more conventional model-learning technique.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses and areas for further exploration:

3.  **Density and Scalability:** The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process. A significant limitation is that learning full SEs is computationally demanding and currently does not scale to more complex environments like HalfCheetah or Humanoid. The paper acknowledges the significant computational cost of NES and the limitation that 16 workers were insufficient for such environments, which significantly curtails the practical utility of SEs for challenging problems where such proxy benefits would be most impactful. While RNs scale better, the full SE concept's scalability remains a bottleneck. Furthermore, it's noted that "learning an SE proxy requires more interactions with the real environment than training agents only on the real environment," indicating a high initial cost for the proxy itself, which must be rigorously justified against the purported long-term benefits for *multiple* subsequent agent trainings. Future work should explicitly address methods to reduce this computational burden or explore more gradient-efficient meta-optimization techniques.

4.  **Hypotheses and Transferability Nuances:** Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine): "Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**." It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are *generally* not very informative, and the *useful* -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effectiveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis. Relatedly, the claim that observed distribution shifts are "more likely generated by the SE than the agents itself" is a weak inference based on visual alignment, not a rigorous analysis. Such shifts could indicate an SE that poorly models the true environment, leading to agents exploiting artifacts rather than learning generalizable policies. While the method is described as agent-agnostic, the empirical results for transferring SEs to TD3 agents show 'limited transferability' and a high failure rate. The paper's admission of "limited transferability" for TD3, followed by selecting "five best SE models" to demonstrate improved performance, can be seen as a form of cherry-picking that misrepresents the overall robustness and transferability of the learned SEs. This suggests that while the meta-learning objective is agent-agnostic, the *learned proxy* might still be somewhat biased towards the agent family used during its training. This nuance should be highlighted more prominently in the main text's discussion of contributions, indicating that if only a small subset of models works, the general claim of transferability is weakened.

5.  **Methodological and Presentation Details:**
    *   **Baselines:** While the paper compares SE-trained agents to agents trained on the real environment, it would be valuable to include a model-based RL baseline (e.g., training an agent on a dynamics model learned via supervised learning, as discussed in Appendix D, directly in the main text) to further contextualize the SE performance beyond just speed-up against the real environment. A more comprehensive comparison against state-of-the-art model-based RL methods or other meta-learning approaches for environment generation is largely absent for SEs.
    *   **RN Objective:** The RN optimization objective `ntr + wsol * max(0, Csol - Cfin)` (Section 4) is introduced without extensive justification for its specific form and weighting. While Appendix B.4 studies alternatives, a clearer initial motivation for this specific composite objective would be beneficial, and the sensitivity of results to this fixed weight could be further explored. The design of the RN objective function could be refined to encourage continuous improvement beyond task-solving thresholds.
    *   **Theoretical Guarantees:** For SEs and the non-potential RN variants, there are no theoretical guarantees akin to Ng et al.'s PBRS. While the paper argues for potentially better performance with fewer constraints, a deeper theoretical understanding of the properties of the learned dynamics/rewards would strengthen the work.
    *   **Algorithm 1 Details:** The description of the NES update rule in Algorithm 1, specifically Line 8, appears to be a simplified gradient estimate rather than a full Natural Evolution Strategy update, which typically adapts the covariance matrix. This needs clarification and justification, especially given that the noise standard deviation (σ) is listed as an optimized hyperparameter (Table 3).
    *   **Early Stopping Heuristic (Appendix A.1):** The heuristic `| ¯Cd− ¯C2d| / | ¯C2d| ≤ Cdif f` for early stopping agent training is problematic. If `| ¯C2d|` is zero or very close to zero (e.g., in sparse reward environments before any reward is collected), this division is undefined or highly unstable. A more robust formulation is essential.
    *   **Inconsistent HP Sampling:** Section 5 states agent HPs are sampled "after each outer loop iteration" and "before each TrainAgent call," which creates ambiguity. A clear, consistent description of when HPs are varied is required.
    *   **Gumbel-Softmax for TD3:** The use of Gumbel-Softmax for discrete actions with TD3 is a common approximation but should be acknowledged as a potential factor in the observed "limited transferability," as it may introduce specific learning dynamics not present in continuous TD3 or other discrete-action algorithms.
    *   **Reward Learning Claim:** Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).
    *   **Further Analysis of RN Variants:** The paper notes that 'additive non-potential RN (r + Φ(s′)) seems to often be the better choice' and that 'exclusive potential RN (γΦ(s′) − Φ(s)) shows an increased risk of failing' (Section 6). A more detailed analysis or hypothesis for these observed behaviors could provide deeper insights into the inductive biases of different reward shaping approaches within their framework.

### Nits / Additional comments

*   Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).
*   Equation (1) Notation: The `arg max` operator is missing the variable over which the maximization is performed (e.g., `arg max_θ`).
*   Figure 1 Clarity: The data flow in Figure 1 for SEs, particularly the 'freezepolicy' and 'trainagent' interaction, could be clearer to explicitly denote inner vs. outer loop interactions and avoid confusion with real environment interaction claims.
*   Reproducibility URL: The GitHub URL provided (`https://github.com/automl/learning environments`) contains a space, rendering it invalid. This is a critical error for reproducibility and should be corrected immediately.
*   Internal Cross-References: The paper repeatedly refers to "Section 9" (e.g., in Section 5 and Appendix A.4) which does not exist in the manuscript. This likely refers to Section 5, indicating a significant oversight.
*   Table Numbering: In Appendix B.3.4, "Table 13" is referenced twice for different purposes, suggesting a numbering error or misattribution of tables.
*   Citation Style: Inconsistent citation style (e.g., "Rechenberg, 1973; Salimans et al., 2017" vs. "(Salimans et al., 2017)").
*   Accent Marks: Accents in author names (e.g., S¨alinger, Now´e) are not consistently rendered throughout the text.
*   Table 15/19 (ICM HPs): For hidden layers, the "Optimized value" is a dash and "Value range" is "not optimized," which is confusing. A default value should be listed if it was not optimized.
*   Clarity in Figures: Some figures, particularly the density plots (e.g., Figure 3), could benefit from clearer labels for the x-axis (e.g., 'Cumulative Test Reward'). The legend in Figure 3 refers to 'HPs: varied (5 best)' which is only explained later and is specific to TD3; this could be confusing. Consider making the legends more universally applicable or splitting the figure. Similarly, the green histograms in Figure 4 and 8 are described as 'SE responses when fed with real environment data', which is an important distinction but might be missed by a casual reader.

Summary Of The Review:
This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, thorough research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the empirical section is exhaustive. While there are some limitations regarding scalability for complex environments, nuances in transferability, and several minor methodological and presentation details that should be addressed, the core contribution is strong and opens promising new avenues for research in meta-learning for RL.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors propose a framework for learning to synthesize a proxy models for usually non-learned components of an RL loop: namely transition dynamics and reward as defined in the interacting environment. They learn the parameters of these model by "meta-learning" onto the real environment with a learning agent (with slight detail variants depending on what the learning objective is), showing that not only can this setup lead to successfully learn the proxy parameters, but also improve learning performance for the agents.

Main Review:
First and foremost, let me start by saying that I *really* like this manuscript.

1. The writing is extremely clear & detailed, and makes the paper a pleasure to read. I also found that the authors tended to answer questions that were popping as I was reading paragraphs immediately thereafter, which is a likely sign of writing that has gone through a lot of careful iterations.

2. The authors have gone through a lot of work to justify the choice of optimization methods, how and why the framework was setup in certain ways, and how the hyperparameters for a now fairly complex learning system were chosen. Considering the code release and the extremely detailed appendices, I suspect the work will be extremely easy to reproduce. Truly commendable, and a great example of what good ML research looks like (I also particularly enjoyed the frank discussion about the work's limitation due to choosing ES as an optimization method for the outer loop).

3. The central idea of the method is relatively simple, but seems to be quite effective; it honestly borrows from a lot of previous literature (reward learning, population-based optimization, etc.), whilst effectively producing a novel take on learning syntethic environment models.

So, overall, I am extremely happy to argue for acceptance as it is. Now, let's look at what I perceive to be some of its weaknesses:

4. The manuscript at times feels very dense, especially when looking at methodology and experimental details, and its understanding is greatly helped from the presence of a sizable appendix. It very much feels like the authors could have a feasibly split the paper into two, one on ES and a follow up on RN, providing more space to experimentally analyse ES and RN separately and make for a cleaner and easier review process.

5. Some of the hypotheses presented to justify the improved agent training feel relatively handwave-y. Consider for instance the following quote (emphasis mine):

>Optimizing SEs for the maximum cumulative reward automatically yielded fast training of agents as a side-product, likely due to **efficient synthetic state dynamics**.

It is implied that this meta-learning approach leads to generally discover better data-producing dynamics for the agents used in the system, but I wonder whether this is primarily due to the structure of the tasks employed in the experimental settings (as state space and transition dynamics of these simple control problems are _generally_ not very informative, and the _useful_ -- policy learning wise -- parts of these MDP are instead both easily discoverable via search and extremely informative). 

In short, I wonder if we'd see similar improvements for tasks / environments where task difficulty is extremely affected by the emerging complexity of the environment, or whether we can actually show that this is a property of SEs / RNs when trained in this manner (which would be quite outstanding!). Although that said, the effecitveness of RNs do seem to indeed point to the latter hypothesis.


### Nits / Additional comments

Figure 2: the labels are inverted (dotted lines should be the thresholds).

Section 6 (but generally in many parts of the paper): it is claimed that learning rewards is generally easier than learning transition functions -- would it be possible to find a reference for this? My personal opinion is that it should indeed be often the case, but that generally it is not true (e.g. imagine an MDP with a small transition matrix but an extremely stochastic reward function).

Summary Of The Review:
This is a good paper. It contains excellent writing, good research, and makes for a great example of what an ICLR paper should look like. The research problem and proposed methods are interesting and well placed in the literature, and the experimental section is exhaustive.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
This paper proposes to learn neural environment models they term Synthetic Environments, such that planning in the model with model-free RL results in good performance in the real environment.
The authors study learning full models as well as reward-only models they call Reward Networks, investigating the feasibility of learning these models as well as their robustness to different inner-loop algorithms and hyperparameters.
reviews.main_review:
This is an interesting line of work and initial investigation. The exposition of the method and description of experiments are quite clear and complete. However, there are a few weaknesses.

The motivation for this work deserves more attention. The authors suggest that SE’s could be used for AutoML, iterating on algorithms in expensive environments like robotics, agent or task analysis, or agent pre-training. However, I’m not sure these are particularly well aligned with the authors’ statement of the problem, implementation, or experiments.
Most applications are predicated on the relative performance of various algorithms, when trained in the SE, being correlated with performance in the true environment.
However, the problem formulation in eqn (1) describes maximising performance absolutely.
This is approximated in Alg. 1 with a generic TrainAgent function that approximates the argmax_theta. But it seems the specific TrainAgent functions chosen are highly relevant to potential use cases – it is not a generic stand-in for an argmax.
The authors certainly understand the relevance of this, as they study generalisation of SE’s to different algorithms and their hyperparameters, and even address generalisation algorithmically by varying hyperparameters during training of the SEs. However, this is introduced as a brief comment in the experiment descriptions, rather than being central to the method.
It feels that to use SE’s for the potential applications discussed, it would make more sense to reformulate the problem statement to explicitly account for different inner-loop algorithms.
Further, simply maximising the final performance of all inner-loop algorithms seems quite limited when applications involve comparing different algorithms.
It might be interesting to algorithmically address this use-case by changing the fitness function to account for the relative performance of different algorithms.
However, it would also be useful to address this empirically given the current formulation of the objective, and the authors likely already have the data to do so, at least partially: does the performance of different HPs/algorithms *in the SEs* correlate with their performance on the true environment?

It may also be possible to motivate the work from a more traditional model-based RL point of view, which might be more closely aligned to the problem statement given in the paper. A closely related work is that of Nikishin et al [1] (who use implicit differentiation to address the bi-level optimisation). NB I don’t fault the authors for not citing this, I believe it is only available as preprint. They argue that models learned only to further optimisation of a policy (as in this work's problem statement) may perform better than traditional models when capacity is limited. However, the ES-based bi-level optimisation is expensive, so it may be difficult to make a strong case for this approach.

Another weakness of the study is the simplicity of the environments used. I understand the authors may have limited computational resources, but it is difficult to know how highly to weight an empirical feasibility study carried out on such small-scale tasks. In the absence of more compelling empirical findings, it is more critical to explore the algorithmic choices and motivations.

In this vein, I did appreciate the investigation of which inductive biases might help during reward learning. Overall, I find the case for RNs is perhaps more intuitive: dense-ifying rewards once in an expensive optimisation to allow fast iteration later. Using an additive potential form even guarantees that this will not change the optimal behaviour – sadly, it seems this type of RN did not outperform the baseline much in the experiments.

In the RN case optimising for the speed of training makes sense, but I still find the chosen objective strange. Why not optimise the area-under-curve returns for the full inner-loop? Clipping at a somewhat arbitrary solution threshold seems like it would result in suboptimal RNs (this appears to have occurred in the half-cheetah experiment in the appendix).

Minor comments:
 - In the SE experiments, it would be good to show the performance of the ‘fixed’ hyperparams evaluated with the same fixed hyperparams: this would show the ‘generalisation gap’ compared to evaluating with varied hyperparms as is currently shown.
 - Overall, many figures are a bit hard to parse. Try using more smoothing or subsampling for curves. For the barplots, maybe re-arrange them so the comparable bars (i.e. measuring the same metric, ‘steps’ or ‘episodes’, are next to each other).

[1] “Control-Oriented Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Differentiation” Nikishin et al 2021

----------------------

Update: the authors have addressed many of my concerns, ran some additional useful experiments, and clarified their reasoning on several points in the overall discussion with reviewers. I still think there is a bit of a gap between the big-picture motivation for the work and the instantiation we see studied empirically; the authors are working on an empirical investigation to help make that connection. However, even in its absence, I am sufficiently positive about the paper overall now to lean towards acceptance -- I found the study interesting and creative.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation. The empirical study is fairly clear but limited by the simplicity of the domains and the restriction largely to assessing feasibility rather than giving clearer indications of how the method could be used in practice.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

While the work is interesting, I find the motivation a little strained at not well aligned with the problem statement or algorithmic instantiation.
